ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CJW (Shrimps and prawns) is the closest parent; no specific code exists for prawns in black pepper sauce preparation.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT 227144002 represents prawn as a food substance; no specific concept for black pepper sauce preparation exists.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical restaurant serving of prawns with black pepper sauce is approximately 300 g, representing a shared zi char dish for 2–3 persons.

nutrient_source.title: Stir-fried prawns with sauce — nutrient profile estimated from IMR Malaysia MyFCD and USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (shrimp, cooked) with adjustment for black pepper sauce components
nutrient_source.organization: Institute for Medical Research Malaysia (IMR) MyFCD; USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for 'Prawns with Black Pepper Sauce' found in IMR MyFCD or USDA SR Legacy. Values estimated by combining IMR MyFCD cooked prawn data (approximately 99 kcal, 20.9 g protein, 1.7 g fat, 0.5 g carbohydrate per 100 g for plain cooked prawns) with an adjustment for black pepper sauce ingredients (butter, black pepper, garlic, oyster sauce, soy sauce, cereal coating) contributing approximately 30–40 kcal, 2–3 g fat, 4–6 g carbohydrate, and 250–350 mg sodium per 100 g of finished dish. Final composite estimate reflects a typical zi char restaurant preparation. Confidence is medium.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index estimated from analogous seafood and mixed sauce dish; reference: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for prawns with black pepper sauce. GI estimated at 35 based on the low-GI profile of cooked crustaceans (GI ~0–5 for plain prawns) combined with the moderate carbohydrate contribution from black pepper sauce and cereal coating. The cereal coating introduces some starchy carbohydrate; however, the overall dish carbohydrate density remains low. Analogous mixed seafood dishes with sauce in the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 support a low GI classification (≤55). GL per serving calculated as (6.8 g carbohydrate per 100 g × 300 g serving / 100) × GI/100 = 20.4 × 0.35 = 7.1.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from black pepper sauce, oyster sauce, and soy sauce
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to 100–150 g and request reduced-sauce preparation, as a 300 g serving provides approximately 1,560 mg sodium, representing 68% of the recommended daily sodium limit of 2,300 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate carbohydrate load from cereal coating and sauce; low glycemic index of approximately 35
type2_diabetes.guidance: The low glycemic index (approximately 35) and moderate glycemic load (GL ~7 per serving) make this dish relatively suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when consumed in controlled portions of 150–200 g, though the cereal coating adds refined starch that should be accounted for in carbohydrate budgeting.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated sodium and moderate saturated fat from butter-based black pepper sauce; beneficial omega-3 fatty acids from prawns
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should be mindful of the high sodium content (~520 mg per 100 g) and moderate saturated fat (~2.4 g per 100 g) from the butter-enriched sauce, while noting that prawns provide beneficial lean protein and selenium; portion control and reduced-sauce preparation are recommended.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Prawns with Black Pepper Sauce is a popular Malaysian and Singaporean zi char (煮炒) dish featuring whole or halved large prawns stir-fried in a robust, aromatic black pepper sauce. The sauce is typically made from coarsely ground black pepper, butter, garlic, onion, oyster sauce, and soy sauce, producing a bold, pungent, and slightly sweet flavour profile. In some restaurant preparations, such as the version served at Marina Seafood House in Singapore, the prawns are additionally coated with toasted cereal (often crushed cornflakes or oats) to add a crispy textural dimension, and garnished with chilli padi (bird's eye chilli) and curry leaves for heat and fragrance. The dish is wok-cooked over high heat, imparting a characteristic wok hei (breath of the wok) smokiness. Nutritionally, prawns are an excellent source of lean protein (approximately 14.5 g per 100 g), selenium, vitamin B12, and phosphorus, while being relatively low in fat. The sauce and cereal coating contribute additional carbohydrates, saturated fat, and sodium. The glycemic index of the dish is estimated to be low (approximately 35), making it moderately suitable for blood glucose management when portion-controlled. The high sodium content from the sauce components is the primary clinical concern for individuals with hypertension or cardiovascular disease. This dish is a staple of Malaysian Chinese and Singaporean hawker and restaurant cuisine, commonly ordered as a shared main course alongside steamed rice.

confidence_reason: No exact entry for 'Prawns with Black Pepper Sauce' was found in IMR MyFCD, HPB Singapore Foodo, or USDA FoodData Central. Nutrient values are estimated by combining IMR MyFCD cooked prawn data with a composite adjustment for black pepper sauce and cereal coating ingredients. GI is an analogous estimate based on the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008. Confidence is medium due to the estimation approach rather than a direct source match.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are estimated composites; no single approved-source entry exactly matches this dish. IMR MyFCD cooked prawn data and USDA SR Legacy shrimp data were used as the primary prawn base, with sauce and coating adjustments applied. 2. Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the source databases for this dish type. 3. Vitamin E is -1 as it is not reported in the source databases for this dish type. 4. GI is an analogous estimate (approximately 35, low category) based on the low-GI profile of cooked crustaceans and mixed seafood sauce dishes from the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008; no direct GI measurement exists for this specific dish. 5. The input food_group 'Oils, seasonings and condiments' has been corrected to 'Fish and seafood' as the primary ingredient is prawns. 6. The input description contained a likely OCR error ('cuTy leaves' corrected to 'curry leaves'). 7. Halal status is listed as true based on the typical preparation; however, individual restaurant sourcing should be verified. 8. Cholesterol content (~148 mg per 100 g) is notable; individuals with cardiovascular disease should be aware.
